chunk_summary: Guidelines for preparing F-75 and F-100 therapeutic feeds with electrolyte fortification to manage severe acute malnutrition, emphasizing safety, hygiene, and proper supplementation.
learning_objectives: Understand the preparation process for F-75 and F-100 therapeutic feeds, including ingredient ratios and hygiene protocols.
Learn the importance of electrolyte fortification in managing severe acute malnutrition and preventing complications like arrhythmia.
Identify practical ward checks to ensure feed quality and prevent errors in therapeutic feeding.
key_facts: F-75 is a starter diet made from milk, sugar, oil, and water; F-100 is a catch-up diet with higher milk content and added sugar and oil.
Electrolyte fortification requires added potassium and magnesium while restricting sodium to prevent arrhythmia and feeding failure.
Feeds must be prepared with clean utensils, boiled or safe water, and discarded if kept beyond the protocol window to avoid bacterial contamination.
Night feeds and warmed feeds are essential for effective therapeutic feeding and catch-up growth.
Common errors like under-measuring oil and sugar can lead to poor weight gain in malnourished children.
reference_quote: Facility-style recipes commonly used in India include a starter diet (F-75 equivalent) prepared from milk, sugar, oil, and water, and a catch-up diet (F-100 equivalent) prepared from higher milk content with added sugar and oil. — Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Operational guidelines on facility-based management of children with severe acute malnutrition, 2011
document_key_topics: Therapeutic feeding for severe acute malnutrition
Preparation and fortification of F-75 and F-100 formulas
